General:
Upright: Innocence, spontaneity, free spirit, potential.

Reversed: Naivety, foolishness, risk-taking.
Blaster: Fool.  Blaster gets +2 skill points, then +1 Wits or +1 Dexterity.
Breaker: Fool.  Cape gets +2 Skill points, then +1 in a stat relevant to their breaker state.
Brute: Fool.  Brute gets +2 Skill points, then +1 Brawn or +1 Guts.
Changer: Fool.  Cape gets +2 Skill points, then +1 in a stat relevant to their changer form
Master: Fool.  Master gets +2 Skill points, then +1 in Social or Wits
Mover: Fool. Mover adapts to their power over time, conferring minor direct benefits. Increased physical statistics relating to the use of their power, benefits to mental acuity and speed, power-granted skill proficiencies, or custom skills to fully utilise their power.
Shaker: Fool. Shaker gets +2 skill pips, then +1 Wits or +1 Dexterity. May stack into superwits with GM permission.
Stranger: Fool. Stranger gets +2 skill points, then +1 Dexterity or +1 Wits.
Striker: Fool.  Striker gets +2 Skill points, then +1 in Dex or Ath
Tinker: Fool.  Two free skill points, then either +1 Dex or +1 Know.
Thinker: Fool.  Thinker gets gets +2 Skill points, then +1 in Wits or Knowledge

General:
Upright: Power, skill, concentration, action

Reversed:  Manipulation, poor planning, latent talents
Blaster: Magician.  Gain 3 skill points or 5 skill points in one of the following skills: Aim or Barrage.  If taking the five skill points, then lose facility with melee - cannot have melee skills, and on first melee attack of combat, roll an added die and discard the highest.
Breaker: Magician.  Cape gets 4 free skill points that they can allocate whenever they change forms.  These points are only available while transformed.
Brute:
Magician.  The brute gains either +3 or +5 skill points in Brawl, Blitz, Intimidate, Endurance, Willpower or Withstand.

If they took the +3 instead of the +5, then they gain the ability to Roar.  Roaring is an attack action and rattles the senses of everyone in earshot (alternately, can roar at foes in one direction, leaving allies behind the brute unaffected).  The next time those affected would use a skill, they must pass a Guts check, or gain no benefit from the skill.  Can be light or vibration if such suits the power.

If the brute has a power that affects wide areas and isn’t perpetually active, they can choose to instead apply the ‘roar’ effect to that power.
Changer: Magician.  Cape gets 4 free skill points that they can allocate whenever they change forms.  These points are only available while transformed.
Master:
Magician.  The Master gains either +3 or +5 skill points in Reflexes, Tactics, Awareness, Command, Deception or Willpower.

If they took the +3 instead of the +5, gain the ability to shunt one wound from master to minion or vice versa on a moderate cooldown.
Mover:
Magician. Mover gains proficiency in a skill that relates to the use of their power, or the power enhances the use of their other skills. Can verge on being a minor skill thinker power.

Alternatively, the mover gains the ability to activate their power reflexively when in danger or gain enhanced ability with their movement when threatened.
Shaker: Magician. Gain 3 or 5 skill points in a ranged skill that the Shaker uses. If you chose 3, may take an additional spec within that skill. If you already have max skill points in that skill, may be allowed to redistribute.
Stranger: Magician. Stranger gets +3 or +5 skill points in Sneak, Deception, Evade, or Thievery. If the stranger took the +3 skill points, they gain the ability to inflict wounds of greater severity on targets affected by their powers.
Striker:
Magician.  The striker gains either 3 or 5 skill points in Finesse, Blitz, Strongarm, Evade or Acrobatics.
If they took the 3 skill points instead of the 5, then they gain the ability to apply pain to their touches, as with the fist spec.
Tinker: Magician.  Either master (That is, have 5 ranks in) the Crafting skill or Computer skill.  All things created and done are touched by the tinker classification and distinct as the character’s own.
Thinker: Magician.  The Thinker gains either +3 or +5 skill points to spend as they choose.  If they took the +3 points, they may take an additional specialty over their regular cap.

General:
Upright: Intuition, higher powers, mystery, subconscious

Reversed:  Hidden agendas, need help to listen to inner voice
Blaster: Priestess.  The passenger and the blaster are in sync, operating on similar wavelengths.  With this augment and with each milestone they achieve, the blaster can expand their options; they either gain a new variant on the manner in which they can shoot their blasts, often similar to the first (see secondary blaster powers, above), or they gain a variant on what they shoot (from fire to sparks or snow to ice spears).  Over time, they build a repertoire of options.  Further, from here on out, if they would roll to determine what they improve or what suffers, (ie.  improving power, accuracy, scope, versatility, etc) they can pick instead of rolling.  When rolling on this table, they can choose to roll twice and pick one option, though they do not pick the sub-option, or they can choose to pick the sub-option they prefer).
Breaker:
Priestess.  The passenger and the cape are in sync, operating on similar wavelengths.  When the cape would gain a secondary power, roll an augment, or roll a power perk, they roll an additional time and discard an option.

Alternately, the passenger drives the cape forward, working to ensure that the cape can survive longer in combat.  Their alternate form gains an amount of natural armor or an alternative small defensive boost as decided by the GM.
Brute:
Priestess.  The passenger and the brute are in sync, operating on similar wavelengths.  When the brute would gain a secondary power, roll an augment, or roll a power perk, they roll an additional time and discard an option.

Alternately, the passenger drives the brute forward, working to ensure that the brute dies in conflict, not in ignominy.  The brute gains two points of armor, and damage taken to this armor comes with no effects or penalties, but this is a special reserve representing the passenger’s focus, and applies only to wounds taken when the brute isn’t being actively observed.  It serves to protect the brute from collapsing structures, traps, and other hazards.  When the dust clears, the brute stands tall.
Changer:
Priestess.  The passenger and the cape are in sync, operating on similar wavelengths.  When the cape would gain a secondary power, roll an augment, or roll a power perk, they roll an additional time and discard an option.

Alternately, the passenger drives the cape forward, working to ensure that the cape can survive longer in combat.  Their alternate form gains an amount of natural armor or an alternative small defensive boost as decided by the GM.
Master: Priestess.  The passenger and the Master are in sync, operating on similar wavelengths.  When the Master or minion gains a new ability, the other gains the ability at a lesser rate.  Further, when rolling on this table, they can choose to roll twice and pick one option.
Mover:
Priestess. The passenger and the mover are in sync, operating on similar wavelengths. When the mover would gain a secondary power, roll an augment, or roll a power perk, they roll an additional time and discard an option.

Further, from here on out, the mover may gain slight bonuses to the scope, power, range, accuracy, or versatility of their movement abilities in addition to the normal bonuses that a perk or augment would provide. The bonuses are decided by the perk or augment they reject when picking from the two rolled.
Shaker: Priestess. The Passenger and the Shaker are operating on similar wavelengths. They gain slightly more power at each shardrep level, which may be represented by gaining the option to, whenever they would roll on this table, roll twice and pick one. May also simply gain more element options as they gain shardrep.
Stranger:
Priestess.The passenger and the stranger are in sync, operating on similar wavelengths. When the stranger would gain a secondary power, roll an augment, or roll a power perk, they roll an additional time and discard an option.
The stranger may alternatively gain more expressions of their power - expressing a shadow cloaking ability as a dark fog, for example - as they focus on one aspect of their kit over others.
Striker: Priestess.  The passenger and the striker are in sync, operating on similar wavelengths.  With this augment and with each milestone they achieve, the striker can expand their options; they either gain a new variant on what their choice of striker effects is, often similar to the first, or they gain a variant on what they can accomplish with their primary striker effect (use for movement, defense, etc).  Over time, they build a repertoire of options.  This limited by your GM.  Further, from here on out, if they would roll to determine what they improve or what suffers, (ie.  improving power, accuracy, scope, versatility, etc) they can pick instead of rolling.  When rolling on this table, they can choose to roll twice and pick one option, though they do not pick the sub-option, or they can choose to pick the sub-option they prefer).
Tinker: Priestess.  The tinker and their passenger are in sync, both doing well to inform the other of what they want.  This is reflected in the tinker having the ability to research where they ordinarily wouldn’t be able to, having an easier time getting research done (requiring less time between sessions), or having more choice over what the research turns up (such as getting two ideas about what the research result is, and getting to pick one).
Thinker: Priestess.  The passenger and the Thinker are in sync, operating on similar wavelengths.  The thinker gains an extra rank of effective superknowledge for the purposes of learning, granting them reduced time for relearning skills, allowing them to take skills above their stat caps and increasing their specialty cap by 1.  This does not grant them additional skill points.  Further, when rolling on this table, they can choose to roll twice and pick one option.

General:
Upright: Fertility, femininity, beauty, nature

Reversed: Creative block, dependence on others
Blaster:
Empress.  The parahuman can channel the energies they would normally display to show off.  They might manifest veins that crackle with electricity, make themselves glow, or have their haemokinetic blood power form a sigil on their chest.  Gain +1 Social so long as they’ve used their blast in the last minute, and are always considered to have their choice (made once) of the Emblem, Icon, or Unique costume features.

Alternately, they gain the costume feature noted above and the option of using an stifled blast to disorient or blind foes; this is a 10’ range attack, using Dex vs.  the enemy’s Wits check, and after use, the Blaster can’t use their blast for 1d3 rounds.  Another interpretation might stagger & push foes back 5’ per point by which they failed the contested roll, if a ‘flash’ doesn’t suit the blaster’s element.
Breaker:
Empress.  The transformed state has unnatural movement.  Grants access to the feign spec and 2 skill points in deception.  If they already have the spec, instead gives them a small bonus to rolls vs scrutinise as long as they are transformed.

Alternately, the cape has determined how to better shape their transformation for a particular desired effect (Awe, Intimidation or another option).  This could manifest as a small morale buff to allies (Awe) or they can make a Threaten roll vs.  the nearest opponent’s Guts-based willpower whenever they transform or do something particularly monstrous.  If the intimidation is successful, that cape has -1 to all rolls involving the transformer as long as they remain transformed.
Brute:
Empress.  The brute gains the ability to channel energies through their costume to better convey the images they want.  Power ripples through the material to show off, or the brute’s muscles and exoskeleton might grow and fold around costume elements.  They gain +1 Social after a show of strength or taking any blow that would deliver a moderate or worse injury, and can choose the Dangerous, Emblem, Icon, or Style costume feature and have it be a permanent feature of any costume they wear.

Alternately, they can make a Social roll vs.  the nearest opponent’s Guts to intimidate as part of a display of a feat of strength (attacking, hurling a large object, destroying terrain).  If successful, the opponent is afraid and suffers a -1 to rolls that would involve the brute.  Opponents that are already unnerved or who would become unnerved by another means (namely, the threaten skill) are instead terrified and must pass a Guts check (beating the brute’s Social) to approach or take action against the Brute.  Requires another two feats of strength to regain this bonus intimidation.
Changer:
Empress.  The transformed state has unnatural movement.  Grants access to the feign spec and 2 skill points in deception.  If they already have the spec, instead gives them a small bonus to rolls vs scrutinise as long as they are transformed.

Alternately, the cape has determined how to better shape their transformation for a particular desired effect (Awe, Intimidation or another option).  This could manifest as a small morale buff to allies (Awe) or they can make a Threaten roll vs.  the nearest opponent’s Guts-based willpower whenever they transform or do something particularly monstrous.  If the intimidation is successful, that cape has -1 to all rolls involving the transformer as long as they remain transformed.
Master: Empress.  Grants access to the feign spec and two ranks in deception for constructs (even with less than 5 social).  If they already have the specialty, get two free skill points and instead allows them to ignore the first penalty from feigning per person.  This applies to constructs if it does not already, or if it does apply to constructs it applies to the master instead.  Alternately, their strikes are intimidating, pushing attacked targets back and forcing a DC18 acrobatics roll against stagger.
Mover: Empress. The parahuman is better able to utilise their movement to feint, distract, or deceive. This can grant them access to the feint ability, allow them to use the deception skill as part of power usage, create afterimages when they move, give them a ‘fake out’ movement ability, or something else as per GM discretion.
Shaker: Empress. The parahuman can channel their power to show off, perhaps causing fear, confusion, or awe in their opponents, or perhaps staggering or throwing them off their balance. In other words, the power gains an aspect that debilitates opponents in its range.
Stranger:
Empress. While hidden, the stranger has a greater predilection for inflicting negative effects. They could channel debilitating pain, blinding smoke, crushing depression and the like.
Alternatively, the stranger has an increased ability to deceive and fake out enemies, giving them access to the feint ability or pips in the Deception skill.
Striker:
Empress.  Grants access to the feign spec and two ranks in deception (even with less than 5 social).  If they already have the spec, instead allows them to ignore the first penalty from feigning per person.
Alternately, their strikes are intimidating and push touched targets back and force them roll vs stagger (dc 18 acro)
Tinker:
Empress.  The tinker’s gear is aesthetically pleasing.  Armor comes with a free style addition, the tinker starts off with immediately better rep/first impressions, and so long as the tinker is fully geared up, the tinker can apply a +1 to any Social roll in a given encounter.

Alternatively, start with a mask built.  Comes with privacy boosting effects (helping to mask the tinker’s voice and identity) and either bonus immunities and protections against the tinker’s own work (Poison gas for a [Toxic] Tinker), immunity to blindness for a [Light] Tinker) or it comes with a once-per-day (or once per six hours, etc) activation effect, buffing or protecting oneself.  The mask is added to the tinker’s build list and more masks can be built/researched at half normal speed
Thinker: Empress.  Grants access to the feign spec and two ranks in deception (even with less than 5 social).  If they already have the specialty, get two free skill points and instead allows them to ignore the first penalty from feigning per person.  Alternately, thinker has found a way to conceal their movements, giving them a flat but consistent bonus for rolls against scrutinize and awareness.

General:
Upright: Authority, father-figure, structure, foundation.

Reversed: Domination, excessive control, rigidity, inflexibility
Blaster:
Emperor.  The parahuman can channel their power into their costume, provided it is made of an appropriate material (requires Material minor quality).  While they’re so equipped, one of the following applies (picked as this augment is rolled, leaning toward the most appropriate):

They gain the Armor costume benefit.  When their last available armor slot (including worn armor) is broken, they detonate, pushing all foes within ten feet away by 5’ and leaving a patch of [environmental effect, see blaster’s element] behind in the area.  On so erupting, they get +2 to blocks until the start of their next turn.  Once detonated, this augment is lost until the parahuman rests for an hour.
They gain the Combat costume benefit, and their non-weapon melee hits now either inflict a lesser wound in addition to attack damage or they inflict the blaster’s blast effect on top of the standard attack damage and effect.  They gain the Flowing costume benefit.  After dodging an attack in this way, they get +1 to hit on their next attack (bonus lost if they get hit in the interim).  After hitting a foe, the Flowing bonus grows to 1-3 against that foe for the next round.  They choose a damage type (bash, slash, pierce, burn, rend, shock).  They reduce damage from that damage type by one step, and gain their choice of either becoming immune to fall damage or having the ‘heavy’ quality when it would benefit them (reducing distance thrown/pushed).
Breaker:
Emperor.  The cape’s altered form gains the trait of a single relevant weapon, or gains an attack mode modelled after that type of weapon, depending on what is more appropriate.

As a second option, while transformed, attacks are so intense they can’t be ignored, in part because of the augmentations to the capes body. Missed attacks deliver a lesser wound but no effect.
Brute:
Emperor.  The brute can choose a weapon trait and have it apply to all melee attacks thereafter.  They gain an additional trait per milestone they have accumulated/pick up thereafter, but can only have three in total before they must start stacking one (ie.  repeatedly taking added damage to scale up damage done).  Traits can be chosen from different weapon types, with limitations only on ‘long’ weapons, which are only available if the Brute has increased size.

As a second option, the Brute’s attacks are so intense they can’t be ignored, in part because of the brute’s personal augmentations flowing through the item.  Missed attacks deliver a lesser wound effect (but no wound).
Changer: Emperor.  The cape’s altered form gains the trait of a single relevant weapon, or gains an attack mode modelled after that type of weapon, depending on what is more appropriate.  As a second option, while transformed, attacks are so intense they can’t be ignored, in part because of the augmentations to the capes body..  Missed attacks deliver a lesser wound but no effect.
Master: Emperor.  The master has found a way to weaponize their minions, by allowing weapon use, granting natural weapons, or adding new offensive options.  As a second option, the minions gain more autonomy from their master in some way, be that senses of their own, a greater range (if applicable), or the constructs may be able to persist and operate a round after their master is KO’d.
Mover:
Emperor. The parahuman can enhance their equipment with the elemental typing of their mover ability. A weapon could move faster or hit more accurately, a costume could allow them to store and release bursts of element to enhance their motion, or something else as per GM discretion.

Alternatively, the mover’s attacks are enhanced if made during or after movement. The mover’s attacks may gain a bonus to hit if they build up enough speed, or the motion lends itself to a larger cleave, or perhaps the attack hits harder by sheer force of velocity.
Shaker: Emperor. The parahuman can channel their power through their costume. Requires the Material minor quality. May grant a bonus to defense, an extra bit of power damage on melee attacks, or a stored cluster of their power that explodes outwards when shell breaks.
Stranger: Emperor. The stranger gains the ability to channel their power through weapons and costume. This could manifest in hidden blades that only reveal themselves when it’s too late, infused costume qualities that blind, confuse, and disorientate, or even drawing upon the materials in their weapons and armour for an alternate way to mess with enemies.
Striker:
Emperor.  The striker finds a way to channel their power through a specific weapon, determined by the gm and them at the time of rolling this perk.  Depending on power level, the weapon may proc the striker effect at a lower level.

As a second option, the Striker’s attacks extend further past their skin and are thus harder to dodge.  Missed attacks do a lesser effect of the appropriate type, or a minor version of their normal effect (no wound).
Tinker: Emperor.  Master (That is, have 5 ranks and the specialty in) the attacking skill for an available tinker weapon.
Thinker: Emperor.  The thinker has found commonalities martially when using melee weapons.  This grants them a single rank of superwits for the purposes of dual-wielding and multi-attacking.  As a second option, strikes from the thinker are harder to ignore, and may apply a single lesser effect of a relevant type on successful hits.

General:
Upright: Religion, group identification, tradition, beliefs

Reversed: Restriction, challenging the status quo
Blaster:
Pope.  Can choose to apply a small area of effect to the blast.  Foes within 5’ of a struck target must also make defensive rolls vs.  the character’s attack roll (use the one roll), and take half of the damage; moderate becomes lesser, lesser becomes either lesser wound or lesser effect, but not both.

Alternately, if an area effect is already a part of the blast, it gets enhanced, either in damage or in radius.

As a third option, the environmental effect of the power can be enhanced if and when the blaster so chooses.  The blasts leave larger patches of flame, or arcs of electricity reach further when the blasts touch a conductive surface.  If there were no environmental effects to the blast, one is given from here on out, instead.
Breaker: Pope.  The cape gains the ability to move more easily through terrain, for example flickering.  Movement lasts for a single move action, and requires concentration to use.  As an alternative option, the power is more effective at destroying terrain and armor.
Brute:
Pope.  The brute gains +2 Brawn for the purposes of tearing through terrain, or, if they would already be able to, gains the ability to walk through most non-reinforced terrain (up to and including brick walls) without slowing or being otherwise impeded.

Alternately, the brute’s power ripples through to help destroy objects.  When the brute destroys terrain, any foes within 5’ of smaller props (microwave oven to mailbox) or 10’ of larger props (larger than a mailbox, cars, section of wall) stagger 5’ back from that terrain, with the staggered condition.  If the destruction of terrain was a prelude to a follow-up attack, the foe suffers a -2 to dodge.  Thrown terrain or cascading damage from the brute’s earlier actions does transmit this effect.
Changer: Pope.  The cape gains the ability to move more easily through terrain, for example short-ranged tunnelling underneath it.  Movement lasts for a single move action, and requires concentration to use.  As an alternative option, the power is more effective at destroying terrain and armor.
Master: Pope.  The Master constructs can ignore terrain penalties when moving, or else gain a new minor movement option.  As an alternative option, the constructs become more destructive in nature and does bonus damage to terrain and armor.
Mover:
Pope. Terrain no longer impedes the mover as much as it used to. The mover may gain facility in manipulating the terrain with their power, or the power provides alternate ways to ignore harsh terrain and obstacles, or the mover can bypass some terrain penalties with enough movement.

Alternatively, the mover’s motion has a destructive effect on the environment. Accelerated running could leave a trail of fire, while flight could create ripples of harsh terrain.
Shaker:
Pope. The Shaker has more effect on the environment. Their fear aura trails behind them even after they’ve moved, their fires burn an extra bit longer, the dust clouds they kick up stay much longer then they’ve any right to. 

Alternatively, the Shaker’s power might be enhanced in range, covering even more space, or it might break through armor and defenses more effectively.
Stranger:
Pope. Terrain and obstacles phase the stranger to a lesser extent. The stranger may ignore terrain penalties when approaching a target they are hidden from, or gain the ability to use their power to construct alternate pathways or phase through obstacles.
Alternatively, the stranger can infuse their power into terrain, creating debilitating obstacles for their adversaries to face.
Striker:
Pope.  The striker is able to channel their power through the ground, giving them one extra square of reach for the purpose of attacking, but the extra square takes a penalty to hitting.

As an alternative option, the power is more destructive in nature and does bonus damage to terrain and armor.
Tinker:
Pope.  Can build terminals to assist in work, or range/variety of available terminals is extended.  When operating a computer or terminal that is linked to another, to cameras, or to other tech, maintain a general awareness (with own Wits score -2) of surroundings, either in cone or radius, maintaining easy surveillance of own tech.

Alternately, all defensive gear comes with a simple, baseline HUD upgrade providing awareness of surroundings (ie.  radar) or a connection to other systems, in addition to existing HUD upgrades or other benefits.

Whichever option is taken, can also opt to set alarms for when tech is approached or tampered, and gain Alarm trinket for consumable option of same idea.
Thinker: Pope.  The Thinker has found a way to maneuver around terrain better.  They are able to move over hazards without any penalties, and any movement penalties imposed on them are reduced by half.  As an alternative option, the thinker’s attacks become more destructive in nature and do bonus damage to terrain and armor.

General:
Upright: Union, love, relationships, values alignment, choices

Reversed: Disharmony, imbalance, misalignment of values
Blaster:
Lovers.  Gain dual-fire option.  Can fire two blasts in one turn, so long as the targets of the blasts are more than 15’ apart from one another.

As a second option, can a degree of -kinesis, either gaining the ability to manipulate the shot itself or the environmental effects of the shot.  In the former case, shots can be redirected while in midair, which allows it to circumvent cover and makes it undodgeable (can be blocked as normal).  In the latter case, an existing area of environmental effect can be made to erupt (hitting all foes within 5’) or turned into a bolt of energy/material that fires from said environmental effect with a max range of 20’, delivering a moderate wound on hit.

After use of dual-fire or one of the -kinesis options, the ability cannot be used again until after a full night’s rest or after four regular blast actions have been fired at hostile targets.  (That is, not four attacks, but four separate attack options, which may each be composed of multiple blasts, depending on the nature of the parahuman).
Breaker: Lovers.  From here on out, when the cape would target one individual with their primary attack from their transformed state, they can target two individuals, provided the targets are within 5’ of each other.  If they would already target multiple foes by some mechanism, then they gain 5’ reach/radius for that attack/effect, in addition to the aforementioned effect.
Brute:
Lovers.  From here on out, when the brute would target one individual with an attack, manoeuvre, skill use, or power, they can target two individuals, provided the targets are within 5’ of each other.  If they would already target multiple foes by some mechanism, then they gain 5’ reach/radius for that attack/effect, in addition to the aforementioned effect.

Alternately, the first time each round that the brute’s actions lead a foe to be knocked away, knocked down, thrown, knocked out, killed, or otherwise taken out of combat, gain additional options for spending the remainder of that turn.
Changer: Lovers.  From here on out, when the cape would target one individual with their primary attack from their transformed state, they can target two individuals, provided the targets are within 5’ of each other.  If they would already target multiple foes by some mechanism, then they gain 5’ reach/radius for that attack/effect, in addition to the aforementioned effect.
Master: Lovers.  The Master and minions operate best while attacking the same target, gaining a small bonus, to offensive ability or accuracy, when attacking the same target in a turn.  Alternatively, the minion(s) may gain some type of cleave attack to hit two adjacent targets, or increase the range of pre-existing AoE abilities.
Mover:
Lovers. From this point on, the mover gains the ability to actively confer a bonus to allies. An aerokinetic may be able to push allies out of the way of harm, for example, while a teleporter could transport both themselves and a few allies at once. This ability touches upon their typing and is a bonus to positioning, mobility, agility.

Alternatively, the mover may 'pair' with a target at the start of an encounter, granting the two minor benefits relating to the mover's abilities. This grants passive abilities, is low cost to use, and only targets one person per encounter.
Shaker: Lovers. The Shakers effects become more beneficial to a team. They have a reduced or non existent effect on allies, or, if that was already in place, grant a bonus to allies within their range of effects. Perhaps a fire shaker cloaks his allies weapons in flame, or an emotion shaker imbues them with a renewed sense of passion. Either way, the Shaker is better at working in groups and with teams.
Stranger: Lovers. From this point on, when the stranger would affect and debilitate one individual with their powers, they may chain their abilities to another individual within reach. If the stranger would already affect multiple people or depending on the GM, the stranger can instead confer a benefit upon their allies. A shadow manipulator may learn to summon shadow duplicates of their allies, a sight negator may be able to enhance the sight of allies.
Striker:
Lovers.  From here on out the striker is able to choose two targets while dual-wielding, provided the targets are within 5’ of each other.

As a second option - the striker’s energies chain to all nearby targets (within 10’) at reduced effectiveness, or vs a lower dc.
Tinker: Lovers.  Tinkertech automatically excludes allies and civilians from its area of affect, the tinker's gear can be more easily leant out, or the tinker gains a small drone pet or AI helper, stronger and smarter than they would otherwise have.  Essentially, the tinker works better as a team, regardless of what that team consists of.
Thinker: Lovers.  The thinker gains access to a cleave on melee targets, provided they are within 5’ of each other, or they gain the ability to hit two targets with a ranged attack, provided the targets are at least 30’ away from each other.

General:
Upright:  Control, willpower, victory, assertion, determination

Reversed:  Lack of control and direction, aggression
Blaster:
Chariot.  The blaster gains the ability to move and shoot more easily.  If they would have to stand still while shooting, that is no longer the case.  If they could shoot while running, they now gain the ability to shoot and run as part of the same action.  This does not increase the number of attacks they can make a round, but does afford them additional flexibility.

Alternately, the blasts can utilize recoil or the environmental effect (ie.  rocket jumping, bridges) to augment the parahuman’s mobility, giving them more air on jumps or more flexibility in where they can go.  A default might be to add 10’ to their jump height or length.  Use of this ability poses some risk to the user, locks out use of the blast for 1d3 rounds, or the ability can’t be used again until four regular blast actions have been taken against hostile targets.
Breaker:
Chariot.  The shard has modified the power to be more effective at movement.  This could be represented as more movement per action, an extra move action per turn, less movement lost via movement reducing effects(including shifting) or some natural aspect of the transformed state that makes it more suited towards climbing.  This can give multiple smaller effects, or a single larger benefit, as needed.

Alternately, as fits the power, gain the ability to make gargantuan leaps.  Add up the cape’s Brawn and Athletics; they can travel that many squares to a nearby point as a move action.  Foes within 5’ of the impact site receive a negative effect based on the nature of the cape’s altered state..  If they could already theoretically make great leaps, by dint of stats or power, then they extend the range of the impact effect by 10’.  Becomes available again after four rounds, or three successful attacks, whichever occurs first.
Brute:
Chariot.  The shard keeps the brute on course.  The brute’s movement cannot be readily restricted.  At the very least, even if their stats are reduced, leg broken, wading through a sea of containment foam, the brute retains half of their movement speed.  Further, slowing effects are half as effective, and the brute ignores the first 10’ of movement into a movement hampering (giant spider webs) or movement-penalizing (caltrops) area effect.

Alternately, gain the ability to make gargantuan leaps.  Add up the Brute’s Brawn and Athletics; they can travel that many squares to a nearby point as a move action.  Foes within 5’ of the impact site are staggered.  If they could already theoretically make great leaps, by dint of stats or power, then they extend the range of the impact effect by 10’.  Becomes available again after four rounds or after they perform three feats of strength, whichever occurs first.
Changer:
Chariot.  The shard has modified the power to be more effective at movement.  This could be represented as more movement per action, an extra move action per turn, less movement lost via movement reducing effects(including shifting) or some natural aspect of the transformed state that makes it more suited towards climbing.  This can give multiple smaller effects, or a single larger benefit, as needed.

Alternately, as fits the power, gain the ability to make gargantuan leaps.  Add up the cape’s Brawn and Athletics; they can travel that many squares to a nearby point as a move action.  Foes within 5’ of the impact site receive a negative effect based on the nature of the cape’s altered state..  If they could already theoretically make great leaps, by dint of stats or power, then they extend the range of the impact effect by 10’.  Becomes available again after four rounds, or three successful attacks, whichever occurs first.
Master: Chariot.  Movement potential is increased.  The master and minion(s) might have a shared total movement distance, allowing one to move further at the cost of the movement of the other, or the minion(s) might have increased movement on their first turn of creation, or another relevant option.
Mover:
Chariot. Mover has their motion enhanced even further. This can manifest as gaining alternate movement modes, increasing their base movement abilities, or a ‘railway’ expression of their power which allows them to traverse great distances in a second.

Alternatively, as fits the power, gain the ability to hamper or drain the motion of enemies. An electric speedster could sap the kinetic energy of a target to gain increased mobility. Utilising this ability generally carries some risk of overcharging to the mover.
Shaker: Chariot. The Shaker has found a way to move more effectively. This may allow them to move while their effect is active if they could not previously, or to use their power in innovative ways for movement: Controlled detonations, space warping, gravity nulling or more, depending on the nature of the power.
Stranger:
Chariot. The stranger has discovered a way to use their abilities to enhance movement in some fashion. This may come in the form of extra movement allowance, decreased shifting penalty, or increased ability to climb, jump, and traverse gaps.
Alternatively, the stranger can manipulate their abilities to teleport short distances out of sight. A shadow manipulator may be able to pass through their shadow into a different one, for example. Enemies on the other end of this teleportation receive a minor debuff as the stranger’s power emerges from the destination.
Striker:
Chariot.  The shard rewards the striker for attacking different targets.  As long as the target most recently struck with the striker ability is different than the cape struck before that, gain a free action that allows you to shift up to 10’.

As an alternative option the striker can move a bit further when moving towards targets, effectively extending their range.
Tinker:
Chariot.  Gear is portable.  Workstations and other fixtures can be packed up and moved (including, with the help of a vehicle, sections of megaprojects) with ease, and lesser items are easily stowed, though it takes one round of interruptible action to put existing things away and take out items and rig power to them.

Alternately, starts with a utility item offering some mobility (ie.  jetpack, hoverboard, insect arm wall-climbing backpack) or a half-built vehicle.  These items are available on the tinker’s list (expanding or adding to available capabilities) and are fractionally better.
Thinker: Chariot.  The shard has found a way for the thinker to move more effectively.  They no longer take a movement penalty for shifting, and may split their move actions into two separate actions, the total movement of the split actions not exceeding the normal movement they would have otherwise.

General:
Upright: Strength, courage, patience, control, compassion

Reversed: Weakness, self-doubt, lack of discipline
Blaster:
Strength.  The parahuman can defensively blast.  This can have a short-range version, wherein the parahuman reflexively blasts in an effort to deflect an incoming attack.  This requires a Wits check, and on a success, hits foes within 5’ with a lesser wound or weak blast effect while reducing the incoming damage by a step.  Hit foes are pushed back unless they’re large/heavy.  Cannot be done again until four regular blast actions have been taken against hostile targets.

Alternately, the defensive blasts are focused on long-range.  Anticipating incoming shots, the parahuman shoots the bullets or projectiles down.  This requires a wits check (to beat enemy attack roll) and a higher attack roll.  If successful, the incoming attack is negated (if moderate or lesser) or made moderate in severity.  If only the Wits roll succeeded, the parahuman gets +1 to their defensive roll all the same.  Can be done against one attack per round, with no other limitations or penalties for use.
Breaker: Strength.  The natural defenses found within the cape’s transformed state are increased, whether that expresses as more armor, damage resistance on natural armor (either flat or tied to an appropriate damage type) or some other defensive augmentation related to the transformation.  Alternately the breaker can push their defensive form to the limit for a round, mitigating a large amount of damage, but doing so forcibly de-transforms the cape based on the damage taken, possibly imposing a cooldown on changing back if one did not already exist.
Brute:
Strength.  The breaker-like structural augmentations that allow the brute to maintain their form or manifest their defenses flow through their body.  The brute gains a new defensive aspect to their power, potentially in addition to pre-existing defensive abilities.

Alternately, the brute can tap into the shard for a ‘perfect defense’.  They can enshrine themselves in armor or energy, throwing up a better defense that can absorb virtually any blow.  Use of this in time to respond to an incoming attack is a Wits check, opposed by the attack roll.  It lasts for one round, preventing all damage and effects that would hamper the brute, and swiftly falls away by the end of the effect.  Recovery of this effect is slow, and takes one hour or six feats of strength (decided once and locked in as the requirement) before it is available again.
Changer:
Strength.  The natural defenses found within the cape’s transformed state are increased, whether that expresses as more armor, damage resistance on natural armor (either flat or tied to an appropriate damage type) or some other defensive augmentation related to the transformation.

Alternately the changer can push their defensive form to the limit for a round, mitigating a large amount of damage, but doing so forcibly de-transforms the cape based on the damage taken, possibly imposing a cooldown on changing back if one did not already exist.
Master: Strength.  Master constructs become more durable, granting a larger health pool, a resistance to a damage type, or some other defensive benefit.  Alternately, the construct(s) have a minor offensive power that activates upon death/destruction.
Mover: Strength. The mover gains the ability to defensively utilise their power. They may be able to draw on their powers to gain bonuses to evading attacks, reactively move away from danger, reroll a defense, or something else depending on GM discretion.
Shaker: Strength. The parahuman has discovered a way to use their power more defensively. They may be able to release their power to knock back incoming projectiles, to pull their power closer around them to reduce incoming damage, or they may simply be harder to hit while within the effects of their power.
Stranger:
Strength. The stranger has found a way to utilise their abilities in a more defensive way. They may be able to exert effort to gain a bonus on defending, redirect and disperse the damaging aspects of an attack, or enter a state where they interact less with the world and thus take less damage.
Alternatively, the stranger is able to ‘drop out of the world’ completely as a defensive manuveur, negating virtually any way of interacting with them. In this state, the stranger suffers an increasing penalty until they exit. Regaining the use of this ability may be slow, or it might be only once per encounter, but it is not a tool they can play lightly.
Striker: Strength.  The striker and their shard have discovered a way to channel their power through their costume armor more effectively.  This can manifest as a resistance to an appropriate damage type, the ability to sacrifice damage potential for an elemental forcefield, or otherwise, per GM discretion.  Alternately, the shard is more quick to react to incoming blows.  On melee attacks and grapples the striker effect procs at a lower rate in the form of a non-damaging effect in a minor capacity.
Tinker: Strength.  Defensive gear is built with added protection against one damage type, reducing severity of moderates to lessers, and halving lessers.  Alternatively, start with defensive gear built, and defensive gear is always created one tier level higher.
Thinker:
Strength.  The shard has found a way to make the thinker more defensive, be this represented as a conditional bonus to dodging, a resistance to a damage type, or some other defensive benefit.

Alternately, the thinker gains a way to push their power to the limit for for a single defensive maneuver, but suffer thinker headaches or other drawback at increased severity.

General:
Upright:  Soul searching, introspection, being alone, inner guidance

Reversed:  Isolation, loneliness, withdrawal
Blaster:
Hermit.  The parahuman generates added projectiles, which float or form a halo around them, or are effectively juggled or held at the ready (pellets encircle wrists, coruscant halo around hands).  Each projectile requires something from the parahuman to maintain.  They get one at the outset of combat and one per two rounds, but they take a -1 penalty to a stat (chosen in advance) while keeping the projectile aloft and in motion.  The projectile might draw on their physical strength (Brawn), fall to the ground if they move too abruptly (Athletics), require concentration to maintain (Wits), etc.  The parahuman will rarely have more than 2-3.

While maintained, the projectile provides a flat 10% chance to intercept incoming attacks.  It can also be fired as a free action, as an accurate (+2 to hit) projectile that delivers only an effect, while altering enemy defense rolls by -1 per such hit.  It can be fired before an attack action.

Alternately, pick two:
●  The parahuman gets a round’s full rest per 4 attacks made, re-enabling some abilities and options without needing to slow their onslaught.
●  Other augment-and-bonus special attack options that have a timer have that timer reduced by 1 round (including the bullet point listed just above).
●  The duration of wanted effects from own powers (including negative effects on enemies) is extended.
●  The duration or effect of unwanted effects and penalties from own powers (exhaustion, vulnerability) is reduced.
Breaker: Hermit.  The breaker state is easier to tap into, allowing for the breaker to maintain the state for a longer period of time at a cost, or reducing the cooldown for entering the state anew.  As an alternative option, any bonuses or abilities that have a short duration/cooldown upon transformation can be refreshed once per combat.
Brute:
Hermit.  Time is on the brute’s side.  Facing down the brute requires a combination of strength, sustained firepower, timing, focus, willpower and footing, while the brute seems to have an edge in all of these things.  Focusing a power or effect with the brute as the primary target or being the primary target of the brute’s actions imposes an accumulating ‘faltering’ effect on the target.  For each [10 minus brute’s Athletics & Dex] such ‘stacks’ on the target, they suffer a -1 to Ath, Dex and Wits.  They can skip a turn’s actions to swear copiously, panic, curl up into a ball or whatever on-the-spot coping mechanism suits their personality, and consequently shed 1d3+their guts modifier in stacks.  They otherwise lose a stack every 5 minutes.  Willpower's modifier applies.

Alternately, pick two:
●  Every third time they perform a feat of strength or take down an enemy, they recover all ‘cooldown’ abilities, re-enabling skill techniques and bonus abilities.  Other augment/bonus and skill abilities have their timer reduced.
●  Deleterious effects that would last 2 or more rounds on the brute are 1 round or 15% shorter in duration, whichever is more beneficial.
●  Effects and cooldown that operate on a 12 hour or greater cooldown/timeframe are the brute’s choice of either twice as fast or twice as slow
Changer: Hermit.  The changer state is more flexible, allowing the cape to switch between modes faster, or at a reduced cost.  As a secondary option, the cape may scale up their changer form, building up charge every few rounds, giving a small bonus as the fight prolongs.
Master: Hermit.  The master’s constructs are quicker to get back into the fight.  This could be represented as an increased bonus to saves against being knocked out/destroyed (assuming they aren’t naturally fragile by design), a lower cooldown on redeployment or the ability to shrug off disabling wound effects over time.  As an alternative option, individual abilities of minions/constructs have a reduced cooldown before they can be used again.
Mover:
Hermit. Time is on the side of the mover. This could be represented in increased ability to traverse the field and reposition as time passes, alternate options for mobility and defense, or healing themselves while in motion.

Alternatively, the mover gains a suite of abilities relating to their typing that they can build up charges to utilise. Said abilities have minor to moderate cooldowns and are generally supportive in nature, such as regenerating wounds or creating defensive barriers.
Shaker: Hermit. Time favors the Shaker. They can spend actions to power up their ability over time, perhaps increasing its range, power, accuracy, or efficacy. Alternatively, their cooldown effects may recover quicker, or negative effects they inflict may last longer.
Stranger:
Hermit. Time is on the side of the stranger. This grants them increased ability to hide, perhaps represented as modifiers or rerolls against awareness, or more expressions of power over the course of a fight, or gaining charges to be spent on bolstering aspects of their power.
Alternatively, the stranger has an easier time recharging and healing while hidden. They could suppress wound effects, reduce the cooldown of high-level abilities, etcetera.
Striker:
Hermit.  The striker’s maximum power well increases.  This allows them to build a small amount of charge as a partial action and can expend the charges as part of their next attack.  These charges could be related to damage, accuracy, increased utility effects or some other bonus, via GM discretion..

Alternately, pick two:
●  Every third time they strike, they recover all ‘cooldown’ abilities, re-enabling skill techniques and bonus abilities.
●  Other augment/bonus and skill abilities have their timer reduced.
●  The duration of wanted effects from own powers (including negative effects on enemies) is extended.
●  The duration or effect of unwanted effects and penalties from own powers (exhaustion, vulnerability) is reduced.
Tinker:
Hermit.  The tedium of basic day-to-day tinkering is eased.  Maintenance speed is increased, repairs don’t require access to materials, creation of lesser consumables is cheaper, etc.

Alternately, the tinker can build a resource generator.  These generators are mid-level projects, and every three sessions/10 days (GM’s choice) the generator produces a special material relating to specialty.  Gear made with the special material has a tiny bonus feature (a minor quality to armor, or the last round in a fired clip of 6 offers a +1 to hit).  Other tinkers may pay hundreds to a thousand for these special materials, based on how open the tinker can be in selling it and their reputation.  Use/study of another tinker’s special material affords the usual bonus and instant progress in researching.
Thinker:
Hermit.  The thinker has found increased mental processing power.  This allows them to reduce the consequences of overusing their power, or allow them to reduce the cooldown on abilities that have them.

As an alternative option, they may be able to tap into more options that their power already provides over time in combat, scaling up as the fight continues, but suffering increased drawbacks as well.

General:
Upright: Good luck, karma, life cycles, destiny, a turning point

Reversed:  Bad luck, negative external forces, out of control
Blaster:
Wheel.  Enhanced awareness of the element that the parahuman generates with their blast.  In addition to being able to sense [heat, electromagnetics, strong air currents, cold, concentrations of water, large solid objects, metal, wood, specific emotions] and potentially map out areas, the parahuman can ground themselves and negate the effects of confusion.  If the key element is very common, then the parahuman likely cannot detect small concentrations (ie.  held items) or faster-moving (ie.  a running person) concentrations.  If uncommon, they might be able to easily track individuals they’ve struck with their power, unless the duration of side effects have ended and the target took time to clean themselves off.

The second option is that the parahuman can enhance their vision, becoming immune to blinding effects, and gaining the ability to see in the dark (with darkness no longer affording enemies a bonus to sneak).
Breaker: Wheel.  Cape gains +1 to wits for as long as they remain transformed (can manifest as Superwits), and they get a minor secondary thinker power associated with the nature of their transformation.  This could be blindsight, a bonus to awareness, increased accuracy against targets personally wounded by the cape or something else appropriate to the power.
Brute:
Wheel.  Expanded awareness gives a +1 to Wits and a thinker secondary if one of the following is true (picked once, applies thereafter):
Has more wounds remaining than any other combatant.  3 cases of significant property damage nearby, or half of the terrain nearby is damaged.  The encounter has been progressing for five or more rounds.
Alternately, the brute becomes harder to track when one of the aforementioned conditions are met.  Thinker powers may find it harder to look directly at the brute or train a thinker power on them for longer periods of time, or clouds of smoke and debris combined with disruptive power signatures make the brute drop off the radar in the wake of property destruction.
Changer: Wheel.  Cape gains +1 to wits for as long as they remain transformed (can manifest as Superwits), and they get a minor secondary thinker power associated with the nature of their transformation.  This could be blindsight, a bonus to awareness, increased accuracy against targets personally wounded by the cape or something else appropriate to the power.
Master: Wheel.  The master and their minions share senses, granting a +1 to wits, potentially allowing superwits.  Minions gain an additional awareness bonus, relevant to their type.  The second option is shard-assisted vision, granting immunity to blinding effects, and the ability to see in the dark (with darkness no longer affording enemies a bonus to sneak).
Mover: Wheel. The mover gains facility in general awareness of the situation and increased sensory ability, especially with regard to their target. As the mover moves around the battlefield, the reach of their senses widens, allowing them to gain benefits equivalent to a minor thinker ability. Passive Wits bonuses, ignoring debuffs when moving towards a target, preternatural foresight regarding their planned path.
Shaker: Wheel. The Shaker gains more awareness of their situation. This may manifest as more understanding of targets within their range of effects, perhaps allowing them to see perfectly within their effect regardless of line of sight or obstacles.
Stranger:
Wheel. Enhanced awareness of the situation and especially of the target. The stranger may gain a marking ability to more accurately home in on a target while hidden, or they may get a minor thinker ability relating to the elements that manifest in their main power.
Alternatively, the stranger could interfere with a target’s ability to sense the situation. This could be an application of their powers to the area, or an alternative debilitating effect that they can apply to the target. Either way, the stranger unbalances the sensory battlefield in their favour.
Striker:
Wheel.  Enhanced awareness of the element that the parahuman uses when striking.  In addition to being able to sense [heat, electromagnetics, strong air currents, cold, concentrations of water, large solid objects, metal, wood, specific emotions] and potentially map out areas, the parahuman can ground themselves and negate the effects of confusion.  If the key element is very common, then the parahuman likely cannot detect small concentrations (ie.  held items) or faster-moving (ie.  a running person) concentrations.  If uncommon, they might be able to easily track individuals they’ve struck with their power, unless the duration of side effects have ended and the target took time to clean themselves off.

The second option is shard-assisted vision, granting immunity to blinding effects, and the ability to see in the dark (with darkness no longer affording enemies a bonus to sneak).
Tinker:
Wheel.  Minor thinker power gives some peripheral awareness of tinker gear/activations or additional senses regarding the tinker’s field of specialty.  Side benefit is that created devices don’t/can’t harm the tinker, even on a misfire.

Alternatively, start with a lens with an attached HUD upgrade (see tinker’s available patterns).  Any lens created can be given one additional HUD upgrade, and the tinker can swap between various HUD upgrades at will.
Thinker: Wheel.  The thinker gains a +1 to wits as long as certain conditions are met and an additional minor thinker power (or aspect of their main power) that has to do with increased senses.  This could be represented as blindsight, increased awareness, or an increase chance to hit if certain conditions are met.

General:
Upright: Justice, fairness, truth, cause-and-effect, law

Reversed:  Unfairness, lack of accountability, dishonesty
Blaster:
Justice.  Every 7th round of blasting is enhanced in size, damage, and effect, becoming harder to defend against (-1 to defense rolls), advancing the damage done (lesser -> moderate -> one moderate and one lesser -> two moderate -> critical) and extending the effect, where possible.  Count resets after 15 minutes of rest, after which point the blaster must start over.  Foes must be hostile, legitimate threats.

Alternately, the parahuman gains another possible execution to their blaster power, one that makes them more effective at melee.  They either manifest energies at their hand on one turn and can strike a foe with those energies on a subsequent turn (transmitting the blast’s effect), or they manifest it around a melee weapon, imbuing it, delivering the weapon damage but damaging the weapon on impact (weapon loses one quality, is destroyed when it has none).  An attack augmented in this way delivers an added moderate wound and the elemental effects of the blaster’s blast.  They can hold the effect on hand or weapon as long as they wish, but lose it on delivering a blast/melee hit.

Blasters which already had melee or striker options may instead gain increased facility with those options (such as not damaging or destroying weapons, easier attacking).
Breaker: Justice.  The transformed state gets a ranged option, if there isn’t one already, or increases the range or effectiveness of a pre-existing ranged option.  Alternatively, the cape gets a small bonus to the capabilities of their transformation if they have been transformed for a predetermined number of turns, or if they have entered their breaker state a set number of times within the fight.
Brute:
Justice.  Every time the brute blocks an attack, absorbs an attack with their defenses, or is wounded, they gain a charge.  When they have six charges, they can activate one of two special options.  The two options are chosen from the list as a selection that is made when this bonus is gained, and locked in thereafter:
Next foe successfully hit and wounded within the next two rounds is knocked 15’ back and is rendered helpless.  They can only take their turn if and when the brute approaches or targets them in the round following, and can only take a partial defensive or movement action if so.  They otherwise miss their next turn.  The brute can create a shaker effect of environmental alteration within 15’ of themselves.  Foes are pushed to the perimeter of the effect.  Brutes without an element coloring their power simply crack the ground and make it difficult ground.  Brutes with an element may alter the area nearby, expelling energies/material.  The brute can take a free partial action against/in response to a foe they’re beating in combat.  The brute can get a +4 to defensive rolls in functional invulnerability until the end of their next turn.  They don’t generate charges while thus protected.
The charge expenditures can be spent as a free action, even on other individual’s turns.  In questions of timing, Wits is used to react to a declared action.

This ability can be used multiple times in one encounter, but each time it triggers, the required number of charges increases by 1.  It can be used in multiple encounters a day, but again, each time the needed charges increases by 1.
Changer:
Justice.  The transformed state gets a ranged option, if there isn’t one already, or increases the range or effectiveness of a pre-existing ranged option.
Alternatively, the cape gets a small bonus to the capabilities of their transformation if they have been transformed for a predetermined number of turns.
Master: Justice.  Minions gain a ranged option, if there wasn’t one already, or ranged options already available increase in range, accuracy, damage and/or effect.  Alternately, the master gains an additional minion to choose from, related to their existing options.  If they only have a single construct, the construct might have an extra form, or another option that can be chosen upon being summoned.
Mover: Justice. After a significant period of time in an encounter, the mover may choose to activate one of three moderate power bonuses or additional powers that relate to their typing. Gain a ranged blasting option, enhance the mover power, leave a shaker effect in their wake, or summon a minion to facilitate more mobility. Whatever it is, the mover’s ability has set points in time where it increases in power by a relatively significant degree.
Shaker: Justice. The Shaker’s power ramps up as more enemies fall under its sway, gaining a boost in range, duration, damage, etc. Alternatively, gain the ability to focus your shaker effect in exchange for increased power. Might grant a blaster-like,, striker-like, or even brute-like execution of their power.
Stranger: Justice. The stranger accumulates charges for every time they successfully attack someone that is affected by their power or from hiding. These charges can be spent as free or partial actions for minor benefits to specific aspects of their power. Increasing the radius of a smokescreen, making a blinding burst just a little more severe, etcetera. The stranger could also accumulate charges to a threshold where their power gains a significant boost, up to once an encounter.
Striker:
Justice.  Every 4th round of striking (per round, not per attack) is enhanced in size, damage, and effect, becoming harder to defend against (+1 to hit rolls), advancing the damage done and extending the effect, where possible.  Count resets after 15 minutes of rest, after which point the striker must start over.  Foes must be hostile, legitimate threats.

Alternately, the parahuman gains another possible execution to their striker power, one that allows them to be more capable at range.  They either manifest an orb of energy they can throw to proc the striker effect at range, or they manifest through a ranged weapon, imbuing it into a projectile such as an arrow or a javelin.  An attack augmented in this way delivers less damage and the effect at a reduced rate.

Strikers which already had ranged options may instead gain increased facility with those options (easier attacking, more damage etc).
Tinker:
Justice.  Tinker-made melee weapons generate charges, gathering one charge per 3 successful strikes landed.  Charges expire after an hour, either disappearing or recharging a previously activated trinket.  Can be spent within this time window to revive a fallen drone or boost a tech somewhat.

Alternatively, start with a melee weapon built.  Either add melee weapons to list of available builds or melee weapons that can be built can have another pattern applied to them, switching from one pattern to the other with the press of a button (max once per 2 rounds).
Thinker: Justice.  The thinker becomes more efficient at range.  This may mean they have larger range increments, more accuracy, or another option as appropriate to the main power.  Alternately, the thinker gains an extra tool in their toolbox, that they can only access if they have been in combat for a predetermined amount of time.

General:
Upright: suspension, restriction, letting go, sacrifice

Reversed: Martyrdom, indecision, delay
Blaster:
Hanged.  Added versatility, in the form of two of the alternate blaster shot patterns, two alternate elements, or one shot pattern and one element.  However, when used, these options are even weaker than listed.  Damage, range, or the like may suffer when they are used to deliver a blast.  They are not weakened, however, if used against a target they just (within the last round) hit with a standard blast.

Alternately, gain only the added element, and gain the manton bonus for that element.
Breaker: Hanged.  The transformed state gets an element added to it of the appropriate type.  If there is already an existing element, it becomes more pronounced, or can manifest itself in a new way.  This can be combat-related or utility-related as needed.
Brute:
Hanged.  The brute gains an element through which they can express their brute powers.

If they already had an element, it’s related to it (Fire might become smoke, or magma, or sparks, or red hot metal, or steam).  They can toggle between the two elements, with a 1d3 round transition period to swap where they don’t get the benefits of either, or get only the benefits that make sense for both.

If they didn’t already have an element, they can crack their shell or allow energies to bubble forth from muscle, or something in this vein, changing the way they function for a limited time before returning to normal, with elemental augmentation to attacks or Brute effects.  May last a set number of rounds (ie.  3), and persist a round longer for every round they continue to take damage or dish it out.  New elements draw inspiration from the trigger.
Changer: Hanged.  The transformed state gets an element added to it of the appropriate type.  If there is already an existing element, it becomes more pronounced, or can manifest itself in a new way.  This can be combat-related or utility-related as needed.
Master: Hanged.  The minion(s) gets an element added to it of the appropriate type.  If there is already an existing element, it becomes more pronounced, or can manifest itself in a new way.  This can be combat-related or utility-related as needed.
Mover: Hanged. The mover gains an additional element which they can utilise to express their mover powers, generally something adjacent to their base element. A flying aerokinetic could gain a sonic element to their power. The more this additional element overlaps with the initial one, the easier it is for the mover to access it, but the weaker the secondary element is.
Shaker: Hanged. Gain an additional element to your power, something adjacent to its previous element. An ice shaker may develop entropy slowing abilities, a lightning shaker may develop fire powers.
Stranger: Hanged. Increasing versatility grants the stranger an additional element similar to their initial power’s element. A darkness stranger may gain colour manipulation, a mist stranger may manifest sonic abilities. The secondary element is less prevalent than their main element in all cases.
Striker:
Hanged.  The striker gains a secondary element through which they can express their striker powers.
If they already had an element, it’s related to it (Fire might become smoke, magma, pure heat, sparks, or steam).  They can toggle between the two elements, with a 1d3 round transition period during which they gain only the traits common to both.

If they didn’t already have an element, they gain an element related to their striker power, in a lesser way.  (some small amount of damage converted in type or some added utility effect)
Tinker: Hanged.  A set of trinkets, Concoctions, Grenades, or Traps are added to the tinker’s build list, extending the list that’s already there or augmenting it with another sort of option (limited to one of the four aforementioned options).  The Tinker starts with one of the items built, or swiftly builds it at the earliest opportunity.
Thinker: Hanged.  The thinker gains a better understanding of a given aspect of their primary power.  This understanding can manifest itself in a variety of ways.  This can be combat-related or utility-related as needed.

General:
Upright:  Endings, beginnings, change, transformation, transition

Reversed:  Resistance to change, unable to move on.
Blaster:
Death.  Damages armor.  The first hit each turn removes one costume benefit from the target hit before determining if armor, protection, flowing or any other costume qualities apply.

Alternately, gain an execution blast.  Against the unpowered, gain +2 to hit, and if the target has one or less wounds remaining after the blast connects, they lose any remaining health and have to roll an added time vs.  death, taking the lower result.  Against the powered, only grants +1 to hit and foe must have already rolled to stay in the fight (because of this attack, or at some point earlier).  If the attack is successful, it renews in 1d6 rounds or after 3 blasts (option is decided once and applies thereafter).  If unsuccessful in removing that target from the fight, becomes available in 15 minutes or after 12 blasts (depending on option taken above).
Breaker: Death.  The shard knows how to twist the knife when it counts.  Minor negative effects (bleed, pain, confusion, blind, etc) inflicted by the transformed state continue a turn longer than normal.  As an alternate option, gain a minor bonus when attacking people already rolling for consciousness or death, granting a hit bonus, an extra consciousness penalty or something else as appropriate for the power.
Brute:
Death.  The brute’s powers make them a capable ranged combatant.  They can use their physical augmentations or powers to better throw things.  Either they can extend their mass to better hold onto things, or extend structural augmentations into thrown objects to ensure they hold together better, becoming better projectiles.  Gain a bonus +1 brawn per total milestones acquired to determine if they have the ability to throw a given object or individual, how far they can effectively throw, etc.  Gain a +2 to hit with large objects (200 lbs.  or more), decreasing by -1 for every time this effect has been used in the last five minutes.

Alternately, they don’t gain the ability to throw more, but can infuse elements and powers into objects they hurl.  Element affects these objects, and if object is destroyed on impact, leaves a patch of environmental effect in the wake of the wreckage.
Changer:
Death.  The shard knows how to twist the knife when it counts.  Minor negative effects (bleed, pain, confusion, blind, etc) inflicted by the transformed state continue a turn longer than normal.

As an alternate option, gain a minor bonus when attacking people already rolling for consciousness or death, granting a hit bonus, an extra consciousness penalty or something else as appropriate for the power.
Master: Death.  The minion gains increased capability in disabling foes.  They may perform an execution attack, with a bonus against foes that have already rolled against consciousness or death.  As a secondary option, they gain increased damage against armored foes, allowing their attacks to bypass resistances or armour in a minor but consistent way.
Mover:
Death. The shard knows how to twist the knife where it hurts. Attacks made while riding the momentum of the mover power gain deleterious effects. Bleeding, pain, breaking costume features, bypassing armour. If already under the effect of a debuff, the enemy has their status conditions worsened or extended.

Alternatively, gain an execution maneuver fitting to the power. This technique increases in accuracy and damage when used against a target who has already rolled against consciousness or death. Generally, this is a flashy maneuver that requires a superior position to utilise to the fullest extent. High ground, a clear path, etcetera.
Shaker: Death. The Shakers effect degrades armor, removing defenses slowly over time. Alternatively, your aura becomes more dangerous to those already on the edge of a loss, and enemies who have already rolled to maintain consciousness within your aura suffer from debuffs or increased damage.
Stranger:
Death. The shard knows how to twist the knife in the right way. Minor negative effects may have their countdowns extended by a round, or the stranger could amplify the effects. Pain, confusion, blind, the negative effects that the stranger affects touch on the stranger's typing and element.
As an alternate option, the stranger gains a 'panic' option that may be activated upon being detected. This may come in the form of a second way to hide based on an expression of their element, a debuff that allows them to escape, or something else appropriate for their power.
Striker:
Death.  Damages armor.  The first hit each turn removes one costume benefit from the target hit before determining if armor, protection, flowing or any other costume qualities apply.

Alternately, gain an execution strike.  Against the unpowered, gain +2 to hit, and if the target has one or less wounds remaining after the blast connects, they lose any remaining health and have to roll an added time vs.  death, taking the lower result.
